_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The cyclically-adjusted price/earnings ratio (CAPE) divides the S&P 500 by its average inflation-adjusted earnings over the prior ten years, smoothing out the business cycle that distorts a normal P/E. At ~40.7 it sits in roughly the 98th percentile of its 140-year history — more than double the 17.3 long-run mean, above the 1929 peak (~32), and within a few points of the only higher reading ever recorded, 44.19 in December 1999. The empirical relevance is forward-looking: in Shiller's own regressions, starting CAPEs this high have historically been followed by weak or negative real returns over the subsequent decade. The caveat is timing — CAPE is a valuation gauge, not a trigger; persistently low real rates and higher structural profit margins justify some of the premium, and rich markets can stay rich for years.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator measures total US stock-market capitalisation against GDP — the value investors place on companies versus the annual output of the economy underneath them. At ~231.7% it is in a different league from prior manias: the dot-com peak topped out near 146% and the 2007 cycle near 110%, against a fair-value zone closer to 100%. Because corporate profits are ultimately bounded by the size of the economy, extreme readings imply either permanently higher margins or eventual mean-reversion through lower prices. Two honest caveats: a rising share of S&P revenue now comes from overseas (which GDP doesn't capture), and a long era of low discount rates mechanically lifts the ratio — so 'fair value' has drifted higher than the textbook 100%.
notes.concentration: Index concentration measures how much of a cap-weighted index a few names command. Today the top five companies are ~30% of the S&P 500, the top ten ~41% (up from ~17.5% in 2015), and the technology complex pushes ~48% of the index. The structural risk is mechanical: in a cap-weighted index, passive inflows are funnelled disproportionately into the largest names, inflating them further — and the same plumbing runs in reverse, so a stumble in a handful of mega-caps forces index-wide selling regardless of the other 490 companies' health. The caveat is that these leaders are extraordinarily profitable, so concentration raises fragility and correlation risk rather than guaranteeing a near-term break.
notes.yieldcurve: The 10-year minus 3-month Treasury spread is the bond market's recession tell: when long rates fall below short rates the curve 'inverts', which has preceded every US recession in the modern era, typically by six to eighteen months. After one of the deepest and longest inversions on record, the spread has recently re-steepened back to around +0.5 points — but note that the curve has historically un-inverted just before the downturn actually lands, and the NY Fed's yield-curve model still assigns roughly a 31% probability of recession within twelve months. Mechanically the signal reflects expectations of future rate cuts to rescue a slowing economy. The caveat is lead time: the direction is informative, the exact timing is not.
notes.drypowder: 'Dry powder' is uninvested cash held ready to deploy. Berkshire Hathaway is sitting on a record ~$397 billion — close to a third of its assets — after being a net seller of equities for several consecutive quarters, while surveys show ~79% of institutional investors expecting a market decline. The signal is revealed preference: the most patient, valuation-disciplined capital in the world is choosing the optionality of cash over today's prices, which historically reflects a scarcity of attractively-valued assets. The caveat is that a hoard this size also reflects Berkshire's sheer scale and a leadership transition, and cash on the sidelines can equally become fuel for the next leg up — so it is a measure of caution, not a dated forecast.
notes.finale: Valuations set the potential energy of a sell-off; a trigger sets the timing. Through 2026 the seasonal weak phase of the four-year presidential cycle and mid-term uncertainty erode the bid. The specific mechanism to watch is liquidity: from December 2026 to May 2027, lock-up periods expire on a wave of mega-IPOs — SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic — releasing enormous volumes of insider shares that can be sold into a finite pool of buyers, draining liquidity from an already richly-valued market. None of this is a dated prediction; it is the confluence of stretched valuation, narrow breadth, a cautious smart-money stance and a concrete supply shock that makes the window worth watching.
